title: Collatz Algebraic Family Deep Dive

sequences.name: Image of n under the map n->n/2 if n even, n->3n-1 if n odd.
The Collatz or 3x+1 map: a(n) = n/2 if n is even, 3n + 1 if n is odd.
The Collatz or 3x+1 function: a(n) = n/2 if n is even, otherwise (3n+1)/2.
"Pascal sweep" for k=2: draw a horizontal line through the 1 at C(k,0) in Pascal's triangle; rotate this line and record the sum of the numbers on it (excluding the initial 1).
If n is odd then n, otherwise 2n.
a(n) = n if n odd, n/2 if n even.

a(n) = n if n is odd, a(n) = 3*n/2 if n is even.

a(n) = 1 if n is odd, a(n) = n/2+1 if n is even.

a(n)=1 for even n and (n+1)/2 for odd n.

collatz_a006370.explanation: A006370 (Collatz map: n/2 if even, 3n+1 if odd) IS an exact member of the (x-1)^2(x+1)^2 family. It has the closed form a(n) = (1/2 + 7n/4) + (-1)^n*(-1/2 - 5n/4) = n/2 when n even, 3n+1 when n odd. The recurrence a(n)=2a(n-2)-a(n-4) holds exactly because both the even and odd subsequences are linear in n.

assessment: The (x-1)^2(x+1)^2 family contains 105 sequences that all satisfy a(n) = 2*a(n-2) - a(n-4). This recurrence is equivalent to requiring that both the even-indexed and odd-indexed subsequences be arithmetic progressions (i.e., linear in n). This is a VERY common structural pattern. Any function that is linear-on-evens and linear-on-odds satisfies it. The Collatz map a(n)=n/2 if even, 3n+1 if odd is simply one example. 10 of 105 members are trivially classifiable (pure linear, alternating, or constant-alternating forms). This is NOT a deep 'Collatz family' — it is the family of all sequences that are piecewise-linear on even/odd indices. The connection to Collatz is real but shallow: Collatz happens to be piecewise-linear on n (not on iterates), which is all this recurrence captures.
